title: Junior's

infobox.value: 1950; 76 years ago (1950) in Brooklyn, New York City

is a restaurant chain with the original location at 386 [Flatbush Avenue Extension](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") at the corner of [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue "DeKalb Avenue") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), New York City. Other locations include the [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") area and the lobby of the [Fox Tower](/wiki/MGM_Grand_at_Foxwoods "MGM Grand at Foxwoods") in the [Foxwoods Resort](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") in [Ledyard, Connecticut](/wiki/Ledyard,_Connecticut "Ledyard, Connecticut"). The restaurant was founded by Harry Rosen in 1950,[[1]](#cite_note-rosen-dead-1) although his family had run a diner in that location, albeit not under the Junior's name, since 1929. It is now owned and run by his grandson [Alan Rosen](/wiki/Alan_Rosen_(restaurant_owner) "Alan Rosen (restaurant owner)"). The restaurant is known for iconic New York-style [cheesecake](/wiki/Cheesecake "Cheesecake"). According to the restaurant, it was named Junior's after Rosen's two sons, Walter and Marvin.[[2]](#cite_note-provenance-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American restaurant

This article is about the restaurant in New York City. For other uses, see [Junior's (disambiguation)](/wiki/Junior%27s_(disambiguation) "Junior's (disambiguation)").

Junior's Restaurant

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Junior's in Brooklyn (2009) | |
| Company type | [Family restaurant](/wiki/Family_restaurant "Family restaurant") |
| Founded | 1950; 76 years ago (1950) in Brooklyn, New York City |
| Founder | Harry Rosen |
| Headquarters | [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), New York City |
| Products | [Cheesecakes](/wiki/Cheesecakes "Cheesecakes"), desserts, drinks |
| Revenue | $200 million (2005) |
| Owner | [Alan Rosen](/wiki/Alan_Rosen_(restaurant_owner) "Alan Rosen (restaurant owner)") |
| Website | [www.juniorscheesecake.com](http://www.juniorscheesecake.com) |

**Junior's** is a restaurant chain with the original location at 386 [Flatbush Avenue Extension](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") at the corner of [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue "DeKalb Avenue") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), New York City. Other locations include the [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") area and the lobby of the [Fox Tower](/wiki/MGM_Grand_at_Foxwoods "MGM Grand at Foxwoods") in the [Foxwoods Resort](/wiki/Foxwoods_Resort_Casino "Foxwoods Resort Casino") in [Ledyard, Connecticut](/wiki/Ledyard,_Connecticut "Ledyard, Connecticut"). The restaurant was founded by Harry Rosen in 1950,[[1]](#cite_note-rosen-dead-1) although his family had run a diner in that location, albeit not under the Junior's name, since 1929. It is now owned and run by his grandson [Alan Rosen](/wiki/Alan_Rosen_(restaurant_owner) "Alan Rosen (restaurant owner)"). The restaurant is known for iconic New York-style [cheesecake](/wiki/Cheesecake "Cheesecake"). According to the restaurant, it was named Junior's after Rosen's two sons, Walter and Marvin.[[2]](#cite_note-provenance-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Junior%27s&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Our_Famous_No._1_Original_Cheesecake_at_Junior%E2%80%99s_restaurant_%28Brooklyn%2C_New_York%29_P001.jpg/250px-Our_Famous_No._1_Original_Cheesecake_at_Junior%E2%80%99s_restaurant_%28Brooklyn%2C_New_York%29_P001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Our_Famous_No._1_Original_Cheesecake_at_Junior%E2%80%99s_restaurant_(Brooklyn,_New_York)_P001.jpg)

Famous No. 1 Original Cheesecake

According to *GO Brooklyn*, "At the corner of Flatbush and DeKalb avenues in Downtown Brooklyn, there has been a diner run by the Rosen family since 1929. In 1950, the name was changed to Junior's, and it has been serving its famous [cheesecake](/wiki/Cheesecake "Cheesecake") and other goodies ever since."[[3]](#cite_note-GO-3) Current owner [Alan Rosen](/wiki/Alan_Rosen_(restaurant_owner) "Alan Rosen (restaurant owner)") identifies the roots of the cheesecake in [Ashkenazi Jewish cuisine](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jewish_cuisine "Ashkenazi Jewish cuisine").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Rosen worked with master baker Eigel Peterson[[1]](#cite_note-rosen-dead-1) to create the cheesecake known today as "The World's Most Fabulous Cheesecake", based on a recipe that was in the Rosen family for three generations,[[5]](#cite_note-torch-5) a recipe that calls for sponge cake instead of graham cracker crust. In addition to cheesecake, Junior's features deli sandwiches (particularly [corned beef](/wiki/Corned_beef "Corned beef") and [pastrami](/wiki/Pastrami "Pastrami")), ten ounce steakburgers, cheese [blintzes](/wiki/Blintz "Blintz"), and unique [onion rings](/wiki/Onion_ring "Onion ring").

Fans of the restaurant are not limited to Brooklynites. A [Kuwaiti](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") prince was known to have taken several Junior's cheesecakes back with him.[[5]](#cite_note-torch-5) A shrine to the Brooklyn of old, Junior's has become a must-visit for politicians from borough presidents to President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), who bought two cheesecakes and a couple of black-and-white cookies during an October 2013 visit with [Bill de Blasio](/wiki/Bill_de_Blasio "Bill de Blasio"), who was soon to be elected mayor.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 2020, after reviewing the Federal Election Commission (FEC) filings of Senator [Chuck Schumer](/wiki/Chuck_Schumer "Chuck Schumer"), the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* revealed that the Senate Minority Leader had spent US$8,600 on Junior's cheesecakes in a decade of purchases.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Schumer admitted that he had spent a bundle over the years on Junior's cheesecakes, calling them his "[guilty pleasure](/wiki/Guilty_pleasure "Guilty pleasure")". Holding a platter of the famous dessert at a news conference, he quipped: "Guilty as charged. I love Junior's cheesecake so much. It's the best cheesecake in the world. It is made in Brooklyn. I've been going to Junior's since I've been a little boy. And it's my guilty pleasure."[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1981, when the restaurant caught on fire, a crowd of people watching the firefighters started chanting "Save the Cheesecake!"[[5]](#cite_note-torch-5)[[9]](#cite_note-day-by-day-9)[[10]](#cite_note-About-NY-10) The interior of the restaurant was modernized after the fire.[[3]](#cite_note-GO-3)

## Building and future plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Junior%27s&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Building and future plans")]

The building, at the corner of DeKalb Avenue and Flatbush Avenue Extension, is 17,000 square feet (1,600 m2) of red-and-white-striped menus, flashbulb-adorned signs, rust-colored booths and a wooden bar.

In February 2014 the third generation owner Alan Rosen put the building on the market for development as an apartment tower with the hope of striking a deal with a developer to allow Junior's to return as a ground floor tenant. Rosen received offers up to $45M, but that offer wouldn't accommodate Junior's on the ground floor. In September 2014 Rosen took the building off the market after deciding the existing building is Junior's identity.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In April 2015, Junior's announced it would move its baking operations from Queens to [Burlington, New Jersey](/wiki/Burlington,_New_Jersey "Burlington, New Jersey").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2016, the location inside [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") was closed.[[14]](#cite_note-bizjournals.com_2016-14)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Junior%27s&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* Junior's cheesecakes have been sold nationwide through various outlets, including the television shopping network, [QVC](/wiki/QVC "QVC").[[5]](#cite_note-torch-5)
* The diner was featured in the [Brooklyn Public Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Public_Library "Brooklyn Public Library")'s children's book of Brooklyn landmarks, *Brooklyn Pops Up*.[[3]](#cite_note-GO-3)
* In [Tom Robbins](/wiki/Tom_Robbins "Tom Robbins")' 1980 novel *[Still Life with Woodpecker](/wiki/Still_Life_with_Woodpecker "Still Life with Woodpecker")*, Junior's cheesecake is referred to in "How to make love stay".
* In a 2003 *[Making the Band 2](/wiki/Making_the_Band_2 "Making the Band 2")* episode, "[Da Band](/wiki/Da_Band "Da Band")" was assigned to walk to Brooklyn and get a slice of cheesecake from Junior's for Sean "[P. Diddy](/wiki/P._Diddy "P. Diddy")" Combs. They also filmed their single "Tonight" from their 2003 album *[Too Hot for TV](/wiki/Too_Hot_for_TV "Too Hot for TV")* in front of the restaurant.
* In 2010, Junior's defeated fellow New York City cheesecake shop Eileen's Special on an episode of *Food Feuds* as chef [Michael Symon](/wiki/Michael_Symon "Michael Symon")'s choice for best cheesecake in New York City.
* Junior's featured as an impromptu gathering location in the 2014 movie *[The Angriest Man in Brooklyn](/wiki/The_Angriest_Man_in_Brooklyn "The Angriest Man in Brooklyn")* starring [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams") and [Mila Kunis](/wiki/Mila_Kunis "Mila Kunis").
* Did collab in the 90’s with shoe brand, [Lugz](/wiki/Lugz "Lugz").
